notes.anchor: the man under whose defence I learned how to win
notes.note: Vatinius recalls that Cicero, after savaging him as a hostile witness in 56 BC (In Vatinium), later defended him in court in 54 BC. The whole letter plays on the patron-client relationship that defence created.

notes.anchor: The dispatch to the Senate on our exploits
notes.note: Litterae ad senatum: an official commander's report of victories, sent to the Senate to win a supplicatio (public thanksgiving) and a step toward a triumph. Vatinius encloses a copy for Cicero to lobby on his behalf.
The Vardaei, an Illyrian people in the hinterland of Narona. Cicero's runaway slave-reader Dionysius had taken refuge among them.
Narona, the Roman colony and trading town on the river Naro (Neretva) in southern Dalmatia, was Vatinius's base of operations against the Dalmatae in 45 BC.
